annotations.label: People-and-sets incidence puzzle

anchor.snippet: Given a certain number n of people, is it possible to assign them to sets

annotations.note: This informal motivating incidence puzzle has no formal counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bruck–Ryser theorem

anchor.snippet: This last result, the Bruck–Ryser theorem , is proved by a combination of constructive methods based on finite fields

annotations.note: No Bruck–Ryser nonexistence theorem for projective planes appears in Mathlib (only a title in docs/1000.yaml).

annotations.label: Finite projective plane

anchor.snippet: When such a structure does exist, it is called a finite projective plane

annotations.note: Configuration.ProjectivePlane is an abstract finite projective plane (nondegenerate config with intersection points, lines through points, and three points in general position).

annotations.label: Balanced incomplete block design (BIBD)

anchor.snippet: A balanced incomplete block design or BIBD (usually called for short a block design ) is a collection

annotations.note: Mathlib has no block-design or 2-design structure capturing the balance/replication conditions of a BIBD.

annotations.label: Symmetric BIBD (SBIBD)

anchor.snippet: A symmetric balanced incomplete block design or SBIBD is a BIBD in which

annotations.note: No symmetric block design (v = b BIBD) is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Resolvable BIBD

anchor.snippet: A resolvable BIBD is a BIBD whose blocks can be partitioned into sets

annotations.note: No notion of resolvable design (parallel classes of blocks) exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Latin rectangle and Latin square

anchor.snippet: A Latin rectangle is an r × n matrix that has the numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib has no definition of Latin square or Latin rectangle.

annotations.label: Completing a Latin rectangle to a Latin square

anchor.snippet: then it is possible to append n − r rows to an r × n Latin rectangle to form a Latin square

annotations.note: Although Hall's marriage theorem (the standard tool) is in Mathlib, the Latin-rectangle completion statement itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Orthogonal Latin squares

anchor.snippet: Two Latin squares of order n are said to be orthogonal if

annotations.note: No Latin squares or orthogonality of them are defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mutually orthogonal Latin squares (MOLS)

anchor.snippet: forms a set of mutually orthogonal Latin squares (MOLS) if

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define mutually orthogonal Latin squares.

annotations.label: Maximum number of MOLS

anchor.snippet: There can be at most n − 1 squares in a set of MOLS of order n

annotations.note: The n−1 bound on MOLS is absent since MOLS are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: MOLS construct a projective plane

anchor.snippet: A set of n − 1 MOLS of order n can be used to construct a projective plane

annotations.note: The MOLS-to-projective-plane construction is not in Mathlib, although ProjectivePlane itself is defined.

annotations.label: Difference set

anchor.snippet: A ( v , k , λ) difference set is a subset D of a group G such that

annotations.note: No (v,k,λ) difference set notion is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Development of a difference set is an SBIBD

anchor.snippet: The set of all translates of a difference set D forms a symmetric BIBD

annotations.note: Neither difference sets nor symmetric designs exist in Mathlib, so this development result is absent.

annotations.label: Difference set with λ = 1 yields a projective plane

anchor.snippet: then the difference set gives rise to a projective plane

annotations.note: This construction is not formalized since difference sets are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: (7,3,1) difference set giving the Fano plane

anchor.snippet: An example of a (7,3,1) difference set in the group

annotations.note: No (7,3,1) difference set or Fano plane example is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bruck–Ryser–Chowla theorem

anchor.snippet: the parameter set must satisfy the Bruck–Ryser–Chowla theorem

annotations.note: The Bruck–Ryser–Chowla necessary conditions for symmetric designs are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hadamard matrix

anchor.snippet: An Hadamard matrix of order m is an m × m matrix H whose entries are ±1

annotations.note: Mathlib's Matrix.hadamard is the Hadamard (entrywise) product of matrices, not the ±1 Hadamard matrix design object.

annotations.label: Order of a Hadamard matrix is a multiple of 4

anchor.snippet: then m must be a multiple of 4

annotations.note: The order-divisible-by-4 result is absent since Hadamard matrices are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hadamard 2-design

anchor.snippet: The resulting 0–1 matrix M is the incidence matrix of a symmetric

annotations.note: The Hadamard 2-design construction is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pairwise balanced design (PBD)

anchor.snippet: A pairwise balanced design (or PBD) is a set X together with a family of subsets

annotations.note: No pairwise balanced design structure is defined in Mathlib (the index-1 case resembles a HasLines configuration but PBDs proper are not formalized).

annotations.label: Fisher's inequality for PBDs

anchor.snippet: Fisher's inequality holds for PBDs

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the index-1 (linear-space) case |P| ≤ |L| as Configuration.HasLines.card_le, but not Fisher's inequality for general PBDs.

annotations.label: Erdős–De Bruijn theorem

anchor.snippet: This result also generalizes the famous Erdős–De Bruijn theorem

annotations.note: Configuration.HasLines.card_le states that a nondegenerate configuration with a unique line through any two points has |P| ≤ |L|, which is the De Bruijn–Erdős incidence-geometry theorem.

annotations.label: Balanced ternary design (BTD)

anchor.snippet: A balanced ternary design BTD( V , B ; ρ 1 , ρ 2 , R ; K , Λ) is an arrangement of V elements into B multisets

annotations.note: Balanced ternary designs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: BTD(4,8;2,3,8;4,6)

anchor.snippet: For example, one of the only two nonisomorphic BTD(4,8;2,3,8;4,6)s

annotations.note: This specific BTD example is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Balanced tournament design

anchor.snippet: A balanced tournament design of order n (a BTD( n )) is an arrangement of all the distinct unordered pairs

annotations.note: Balanced tournament designs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: BTD(3)

anchor.snippet: An example of a BTD(3) is given by

annotations.note: This BTD(3) example is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Columns of a BTD give a 1-factorization

anchor.snippet: The columns of a BTD( n ) provide a 1-factorization of the complete graph

annotations.note: Neither balanced tournament designs nor 1-factorizations of complete graphs are formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Frequency square (F-square)

anchor.snippet: A frequency square ( F -square) is a higher order generalization of a Latin square

annotations.note: Frequency squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Orthogonal frequency squares

anchor.snippet: are orthogonal if every ordered pair

annotations.note: Orthogonality of frequency squares is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hall triple system (HTS)

anchor.snippet: Hall triple systems (HTSs) are Steiner triple systems (STSs) (but the blocks are called lines )

annotations.note: Neither Steiner triple systems nor Hall triple systems are defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Affine space AG(n,3) as an HTS

anchor.snippet: Any affine space AG( n ,3) gives an example of an HTS.

annotations.note: Mathlib has general affine space theory but not the HTS structure on AG(n,3).

annotations.label: Number of points of an HTS

anchor.snippet: The number of points of an HTS is 3 m for some integer m ≥ 2.

annotations.note: This 3^m point count for HTSs is absent since HTSs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Steiner triple systems and Steiner quasigroups

anchor.snippet: Every Steiner triple system is equivalent to a Steiner quasigroup

annotations.note: Neither Steiner triple systems nor quasigroups are defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Howell design

anchor.snippet: A Howell design , H( s ,2 n ) (on symbol set S ) is an s × s array such that

annotations.note: Howell designs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.note: Neither Howell designs nor Room squares are formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lotto design

anchor.snippet: An ( n , k , p , t )-lotto design is an n -set V of elements together with a set β

annotations.note: Lotto designs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hungarian Lottery lotto design values

anchor.snippet: The Hungarian Lottery is a (90,5,5, t )-lotto design

annotations.note: This lottery example is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mendelsohn design

anchor.snippet: Mendelsohn design , or MD( v , k , λ ), is a v -set V and a collection β

annotations.note: Mendelsohn designs are not defined in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: If ( Q ,∗) is an idempotent semisymmetric quasigroup

annotations.note: Neither quasigroups nor Mendelsohn triple systems are formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quasi-3 design

anchor.snippet: A quasi-3 design is a symmetric design (SBIBD) in which each triple of blocks intersect

annotations.note: Quasi-3 designs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quasi-symmetric design

anchor.snippet: design D is quasi-symmetric with intersection numbers x and y

annotations.note: Quasi-symmetric designs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quasisymmetric designs give strongly regular graphs

anchor.snippet: Every quasisymmetric block design gives rise to a strongly regular graph

annotations.note: Mathlib defines strongly regular graphs (SimpleGraph.IsSRGWith) but not quasisymmetric designs nor this construction.

annotations.label: Self-orthogonal code from a quasisymmetric design

anchor.snippet: generates a binary self-orthogonal code

annotations.note: This self-orthogonal code construction from quasisymmetric designs is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Spherical design

anchor.snippet: A spherical design is a finite set X of points in a ( d − 1)-dimensional sphere

annotations.note: Spherical designs are not defined in Mathlib (sphere geometry exists but not the averaging/design condition).

annotations.label: Tuscan-k rectangle

anchor.snippet: An r × n tuscan- k rectangle on n symbols has r rows and n columns such that

annotations.note: Tuscan-k rectangles are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tuscan, Florentine, Roman and Vatican squares

anchor.snippet: these are referred to as Tuscan squares

annotations.note: These row-complete-square variants are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tuscan squares and Hamiltonian path decompositions

anchor.snippet: A tuscan square on n symbols is equivalent to a decomposition of the complete graph

annotations.note: The Tuscan-square/Hamiltonian-path-decomposition equivalence is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: t-wise balanced design (tBD)

anchor.snippet: A t-wise balanced design (or t BD) of type t − ( v ,K, λ ) is a v -set X

annotations.note: t-wise balanced designs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.note: Weighing matrices are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Youden square

anchor.snippet: A Youden square is a k × v rectangular array

annotations.note: Youden squares are not defined in Mathlib.
